notes: The same name translated here as "Joshua" has been translated as "Jeshua" in other references. (As examples, see 2 Chronicles 31:15; Ezra 5:2; Nehemiah 3:19.) Ellis Rasmussen has commented on the significance of this name: You may see in this vision of Joshua the high priest of the time (called Jeshua in Ezra and Nehemiah -e.g. Ezra 5:2) a prophecy of the cleansing or restoration of the Priesthood, and of the coming of the

Most scholars agree that the Joshua referred to here was the high priest of the time. But in typical Jewish fashion, there is dualism in this chapter. Joshua (Hebrew, Yeshua, Greek Hee-ay-sous, English, Jesus) was a type of Jesus Christ, the great High Priest (see Hebrews 4:14). The chapter is therefore definitely messianic in nature.
standing at his right hand--the usual position of a prosecutor or accuser in court, as the left hand was the position of the defendant (Psalms 109:6).
FOURTH VISION. Joshua the high priest before the angel of Jehovah; accused by Satan, but justified by Jehovah through Messiah the coming Branch.

quotes.text: Messiah (the "BRANCH"); also of the purification of Israel in preparation for the Millennium, which seems to be hinted in verse 10. . . . The name Jesus is English spelling of the Latin form of the Greek form of the Hebrew name Jeshua, or Yeshua, which means "salvation"! (IOT 2:102.)
quotes.speaker: Daniel H. Ludlow
quotes.source: A Companion to Your Study of the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Daniel H. Ludlow, A Companion to Your Study of the Old Testament, 391

quotes.text: This is perhaps a proverbial expression. Israel as a nation had been rescued from the furnace of Babylon (cf. Amos 4:11) to become a torch to enlighten the nations.
quotes.speaker: Guthrie and Motyer
quotes.attr: Guthrie and Motyer, New Bible Commentary, p. 791

notes: The garment of filthiness represents sin. Changing the garment symbolizes doing away with the old and putting on the new-which would be robes of righteousness.

quotes.text: Joshua is opposed by Satan [see Zechariah 3:1], … not on account of any personal offences either in his private or his domestic life, but in his official capacity as high priest, and for sins which were connected with his office, or for offences which would involve the nation [Leviticus 4:3]; though not as the bearer of the sins of the people before the Lord, but as laden with his own and his people's sins. The dirty clothes, which he had on, point to this [Zechariah 3:3].
quotes.speaker: Keil and Delitzsch

notes: 4:1-14. FIFTH VISION. The golden candlestick and the two olive trees. The temple shall be completed by the aid of God's Spirit.
